# v0.115 ‚Äî function contracts `requires`/`ensures`: preconditions and postconditions as always-on invariants, checked at the dev call site and generated by the runner (testing track, slice 3)

- **Scope:** a single-increment **language + runtime** change ‚Äî the fourth testing-track
  slice and the rung that makes the *invariant‚Üîtest duality* real for pure functions. It
  adds `requires <name>: <pred>` / `ensures <name>: <pred>` clauses on a `fn` signature
  (with `result` bound in `ensures`), enforced at **two** points: a **dev/test-build
  call-site guard** (a runtime check on entry/exit, stripped from release builds) and a
  **runner attack** (the test runner generates arguments over the parameter domains,
  filtered by `requires`, and checks `ensures` ‚Äî a contract is a property that is always
  on). Version ‚Üí **v0.115**.
- **Realises:** the testing track's Pillar ‚Äî *a claim about one result is a contract,
  checked everywhere for free; a property earns its keep only when the claim is
  relational or spans calls* (`design/tracks/testing.md`). `ensures` is the per-call
  postcondition; `requires` is the precondition that both guards the call and **filters
  generation**. It is the same one predicate surface (ADR 0144): a contract clause *is*
  the invariant predicate, now attached to a function.
- **Relates:** ADR 0144 (one predicate surface ‚Äî a contract clause is the invariant
  predicate verbatim); the invariant machinery that already checks agent snapshot
  predicates at the commit boundary (contracts reuse it, scoped to a function); the
  generation engine (v0.114 ‚Äî `requires` filters the same refinement-domain inhabitant
  space `for all` draws from, exactly as a `where` does); ADR 0127
  (capability-requirement *provenance* ‚Äî the source-level `@requires` annotation, a
  **different** construct: it names the capabilities a handler needs and is rejected when
  derivable. The value contract added here is a bare clause over parameters/`result`, not
  a capability annotation ‚Äî the two must not be conflated).
- **ADR at implementation:** one, numbered at authoring (next free after 0149; do not
  pre-allocate): **D** ‚Äî *contracts are invariants for functions.* `requires`/`ensures`
  reuse the invariant predicate; `requires` filters generation, `ensures` is the target.
  The ADR also settles **DECISION J** (the runtime-default question) as one of its
  decisions: contracts run as **per-build-profile** guards ‚Äî on in dev/test, off in
  release. Cites ADR 0144.

## The surface

A contract lives on the function, between the return type and the body ‚Äî one or more
named `requires` (preconditions) and `ensures` (postconditions), each a pure `Bool`
predicate in the one predicate surface:

```bynk
-- commerce/money.bynk
fn discount(p: Price, pct: Percent) -> Price
  requires pct_in_range: pct >= 0 and pct <= 100
  ensures  never_above_input: result <= p
  ensures  zero_pct_is_identity: pct == 0 implies result == p
{ ... }
```

- **`requires <name>: <pred>`** ‚Äî a precondition over the parameters. The predicate is
  pure `Bool`; the parameters are in scope; `result` is **not** (`bynk.contract.result_in_requires`).
- **`ensures <name>: <pred>`** ‚Äî a postcondition over the parameters **and** `result`,
  the contextual binding for the function's return value (its type is the declared return
  type; for an `Effect[T]` return, `result` is the awaited `T`). `result` outside an
  `ensures` is an ordinary identifier (contextual, like `old`/`new` will be for
  `transition`), so it never becomes a reserved word.
- Clauses are **named** like invariants (the name rides the failure report and dedup),
  ordered `requires` before `ensures` before the body, and reuse the invariant predicate
  grammar exactly: `implies`, `is` with narrowed bindings, operators, pure methods ‚Äî no
  effects, capabilities, `expect`, or `Val` inside a predicate
  (`bynk.contract.impure_predicate`, `bynk.contract.not_bool`).

Contracts attach to **pure `fn`s** in this slice ‚Äî the construct Bynk's generator can
attack directly (a function's inputs are its parameters' inhabitant space). Contracts on
capability operations / handlers are a named follow-on (their inputs include the world),
noted under Out of scope.

## Two enforcement points (DECISION D)

A contract is checked **everywhere the function is reachable**, without a single test:

1. **Dev/test-build call-site guard.** In dev and test builds the emitter wraps the
   function so every call checks each `requires` on entry and each `ensures` on exit,
   throwing a contract failure (reusing the slice-1a expected-vs-actual renderer) that
   names the clause and the offending arguments/`result`. This catches a violation at the
   first *real* call, with a real stack. In **release** builds the guards are stripped
   entirely ‚Äî zero runtime cost (DECISION J: per-build-profile, on in dev/test, off in
   release). The guard is emitted once around the function definition, not at each call
   site, so it is O(1) in code size.

2. **Runner attack (a contract is a property that is always on).** For every contracted
   `fn`, the test runner **generates** arguments over the parameters' refinement domains
   (the v0.114 engine ‚Äî boundary-inclusive, seeded, shrinking), **filters** them by the
   function's `requires` (exactly as a `for all ‚Ä¶ where` filters ‚Äî inputs that fail a
   precondition are discarded, not counted), calls the function, and checks every
   `ensures`. A failure reports the case count, the seed, and a **shrunk** counterexample
   with the same reproduce line a `property` gives. This makes `ensures` a generative
   check the author never has to write a `property` for.

The division of labour the track fixes: a claim about *one* result belongs in `ensures`
(checked everywhere, generated for free); a `property` earns its keep only when the claim
is **relational or spans calls** (monotonicity, round-trips) ‚Äî which no per-call
postcondition can express. A `case`/`property` that merely restates a contract already
declared at the source is redundant and flagged **conservatively**
(`bynk.contract.restated_by_test`: syntactic / Œ±-equivalent over the bound arguments, not
full semantic equivalence ‚Äî under-flagging is acceptable, over-flagging is not).

## `requires` filters generation

`requires` reuses the v0.114 `where` filtering path verbatim: in the runner attack the
generated argument tuple is drawn from the parameter domains, then the conjunction of the
function's `requires` predicates is applied as the acceptance filter before the call. A
contract whose `requires` is unsatisfiable over the domain (no accepted case within the
attempt cap) is surfaced as a runner warning, not a silent green ‚Äî mirroring an
over-narrow `where`. This is the concrete meaning of "`requires` filters generation" in
DECISION D, and it needs no new engine ‚Äî only wiring the contract's preconditions into
the same filter slice 2 already runs.

## What changes, against the current code

`requires`/`ensures`/`result` are new; the predicate checking and the generation filter
are reuse.

1. **Lexer / keywords** ‚Äî `requires` and `ensures` become keywords (contract clauses on a
   `fn`); `result` stays a **contextual** identifier (special only inside an `ensures`
   predicate), so existing code naming a value `result` still parses. Guard against the
   ADR-0127 `@requires` confusion in diagnostics wording.
2. **AST / parser** ‚Äî a `FnDecl` gains `requires: Vec<Contract>` / `ensures: Vec<Contract>`
   (each `{ name, predicate, span }`), parsed between the return type and the block, before
   `{`; reuse the predicate/`implies`/`is` parsers.
3. **Checker** ‚Äî type each clause as pure `Bool` in the invariant-predicate context:
   `requires` scope = parameters; `ensures` scope = parameters + `result: <return>`
   (awaited element for an `Effect`). Reject impurity/effects
   (`bynk.contract.impure_predicate`), non-`Bool` (`bynk.contract.not_bool`), `result` in
   a `requires` (`bynk.contract.result_in_requires`), and duplicate clause names
   (`bynk.contract.duplicate_name`). Reuse the existing invariant purity gate.
4. **Emitter ‚Äî dev/test guard** (`bynk-emit`) ‚Äî wrap a contracted `fn` with entry
   (`requires`) and exit (`ensures`, over the bound `result`) checks, behind the
   build-profile switch (emitted in the test / dev-inspect path, stripped in the release
   emit). Reuse the `__bynkExpect`-family renderer for the failure message.
5. **Runner attack** (`bynk-emit/src/project/tests_emit.rs`) ‚Äî for each contracted `fn`
   reachable from a test target, emit a generated check: draw arguments (v0.114
   generators), filter by `requires`, call, assert `ensures`; fold its
   count/seed/shrunk-counterexample into the runner report and the `--format json`
   `message` (shape unchanged, ADR 0098).
6. **Diagnostics** ‚Äî add the `bynk.contract.*` family and the conservative
   `bynk.contract.restated_by_test`; a new "Contracts" category.
7. **Build profile** ‚Äî a `CompileOptions` profile (dev/test vs release) selects whether
   the call-site guard is emitted. `bynkc test` and `--inspect` compile with guards on;
   the deploy build (`bynkc compile`) compiles them off. No new CLI flag is required for
   v1 (the profile follows the command); an explicit `--contracts on|off` override is a
   named follow-on.
8. **tree-sitter** ‚Äî `requires_clause` / `ensures_clause` rules on `fn_decl`; `result` as
   a contextual highlight inside an `ensures`; corpus + `highlights.scm`; re-bless
   `grammar.json`, the corpus, and the embeddable-rule count.

## Spec delta

`site/src/content/docs/book/spec/`:
- **syntactic-grammar.md** ‚Äî `requires_clause` / `ensures_clause` productions on
  `fn_decl`; `result` in the `ensures` predicate.
- **static-semantics.md** ‚Äî the contract typing rules (pure `Bool`, `requires` over
  params, `ensures` over params + `result`, `result`-only-in-`ensures`, no impurity), the
  restated-by-test flag, and the statement that a contract is checked at the dev/test call
  site and by the runner.
- **runtime-library.md** ‚Äî the dev/test call-site guard contract and the runner-attack
  generation/filter/shrink contract (no runtime-library export; emitted per module, like
  the v0.114 property runtime).
- **emission.md** ‚Äî the build-profile switch that strips guards from release output.
- **appendix-version-history.md** ‚Äî the v0.115 framing (auto-banner).

## Docs delta

`site/src/content/docs/book/`:
- **guides/testing/write-tests.md** & **reference/testing.md** ‚Äî a **Contracts** section:
  `requires`/`ensures`, `result`, the two enforcement points, and the `ensures`-vs-`property`
  division of labour (one result ‚Üí `ensures`; relational/cross-call ‚Üí `property`).
- **guides/testing/philosophy.md** ‚Äî extend the spine: contracts are the always-on rung
  between a witnessing `case` and a generative `property`.
- **guides/testing/run-tests.md** ‚Äî the runner-attack failure block (count, seed, shrunk
  counterexample) and the dev-vs-release guard behaviour.
- **reference/glossary.md** ‚Äî `contract` / `requires` / `ensures` / `result` terms.
- **troubleshooting/** ‚Äî a `contract-errors.md` page for `bynk.contract.*`
  (drift-guard the path).
- **reference/changelog.md** ‚Äî a v0.115 row.
- Re-run `build-llms-full.mjs`; keep pages `.md`, British-English.

## Migration (clean-slate; additive)

Contracts are **new surface** ‚Äî nothing is removed or renamed, so no codemod is needed.
New fixtures cover: a passing contract (guard + runner attack green), an `ensures`
violation shrunk by the runner with a fixed `--seed`, a `requires` filtering generation,
a `result_in_requires` rejection, an impure/`not_bool` rejection, and a
`restated_by_test` flag. The v0.114 `property`/`Val` surface is untouched.

## Risks

- **`restated_by_test` must be conservative.** Flagging a `case` that merely re-checks a
  contract risks false positives; the check fires only when the test predicate is
  syntactically / Œ±-equivalent to a declared clause over the same bound arguments. Under-
  flagging is acceptable; over-flagging is not (same posture as v0.114's
  `restates_refinement`).
- **Two enforcement points must agree.** The dev-guard and the runner-attack check the
  *same* predicate; they share one lowering of the clause so a contract can never pass one
  and fail the other. The failure renderer is shared with slice 1a.
- **Release-strip correctness.** A contract guard must be *provably* absent from release
  output (a drift-guarded fixture asserts the deploy emit contains no contract check), so
  contracts never add production cost or change production behaviour (DECISION J).
- **`requires` over-narrowing.** An unsatisfiable/over-narrow `requires` would silently
  starve the runner attack; surface it as a warning (as with an over-narrow `where`),
  never a false green.
- **`result` contextual keyword.** `result` must stay a usable identifier outside an
  `ensures`; parse it contextually (as `for`/`all` were in v0.114), and verify with a
  fixture that binds a value named `result` in ordinary code.

## Out of scope (named, deferred to their slices)

- **Contracts on capability operations / handlers.** Their inputs include the world, so
  the generator cannot attack them directly; a handler contract is enforced by the
  dev-guard only, or by a scenario `case`. Deferred ‚Äî pure-`fn` contracts first.
- **`transition` (step invariants at the commit boundary)** ‚Äî slice 4 (DECISION E/I).
- **Observation (`called`/`never called`/`trace`)** ‚Äî slice 5 (DECISION F).
- **Tier dial + `provides`** (retires `mocks`) ‚Äî slice 6 (DECISIONS G/M/R).
- **History / handler-sequence properties** ‚Äî slice 7 (DECISION H).
- **An explicit `--contracts on|off` override** and **contracts as extractable reference
  documentation** ‚Äî named follow-ons, not this slice.
